questions.prompt: Why does modifier stack order matter?

choices.text: Modifiers apply top-to-bottom in the stack; the output of each modifier is the input of the next. Order determines what each modifier sees. Example: Mirror → Subdivision Surface. Mirror first creates the mirrored geometry (left half from right); Subsurf then smooths both halves. Reversed: Subdivision Surface → Mirror. Subsurf smooths the right half; Mirror then duplicates the smoothed geometry to the left — works, but if you want to edit the original (low-poly) geometry on right side, Edit Mode shows the SUBDIVIDED version, much harder to manipulate. Mirror first; Subsurf second is the standard. Another example: Array → Bevel vs. Bevel → Array. Array first makes 10 copies; Bevel rounds all 10. Bevel first rounds one shape; Array duplicates the rounded — efficient if Bevel is slow on complex geometry. The choice depends on whether you want subsequent modifiers to see the multiplied geometry or just one instance. Each modifier has subtleties: Boolean wants clean topology input (so order Boolean BEFORE Bevel which produces extra edges); Solidify wants to see the un-mirrored mesh sometimes (depends on intent). Pro habit: think about what each modifier needs to see and order accordingly. The visual indicator in Blender's modifier panel — Properties → wrench — shows top-to-bottom; drag to reorder. Common pre-made stacks (character: Mirror + Subsurf; mechanical: Bevel + Subsurf) reflect proven orderings.

questions.explanation: Modifiers apply top-to-bottom; each operates on previous output. Order determines what each sees. Mirror → Subsurf is standard (mirror first, then smooth both).

questions.prompt: You added Subdivision Surface to a character; the face looks soft and pillowy where you wanted sharp brow ridges. Fix?

choices.text: Add edge creases / crease weights on the brow edges. Subdivision Surface smooths everything by default — every edge gets pulled toward an averaged position; sharp ridges become rounded. To preserve sharpness in specific areas: select the edges where you want sharpness preserved → Ctrl+E → Edge Crease → set value 1.0 (fully sharp) or partial values for partial sharpness. Subsurf respects edge creases: creased edges don't smooth; the rest does. Use cases: brow ridges (sharp), cheekbones (semi-sharp 0.5), jawline (semi-sharp), hard-edged mechanical parts (full crease). For broader sharpness control: 1) Add support loops (Ctrl+R) near the edge you want sharp — Subsurf smooths over loops less aggressively when they're close to each other; tight loops = preserved sharpness. 2) Use Bevel + Subsurf instead (Bevel adds edges defining sharp transitions; Subsurf then doesn't over-smooth). 3) Use Mark Sharp + Edge Split Modifier — alternative approach. The crease method is the simplest first option for character faces; support loops are common in hard-surface modeling. The general principle: smooth surfaces from Subsurf are the default; sharpness is opt-in via creases, support loops, or alternate workflows. Most pro character heads use creases on key facial landmarks (brow, nose ridge, lip edges) to preserve definition while keeping cheek areas smooth.

questions.explanation: Add edge creases (Ctrl+E → Edge Crease) on brow edges. Subsurf respects creases; sharp edges preserved; smooth rest. Or support loops near sharp edges.
